speaker: interviewer
text: Take 2, Eddie Ilan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shall I repeat it twice?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's fine. OK, so we got to where World War II broke out and what you did that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I remember planes flying. I never saw a plane before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember there was, like, a gas-- it was a fume, like the vapor from a plane. We didn't know what it was. We thought it was gas, that they were gassing the people. We didn't know. So everybody, they were telling us to take a handkerchief in water and put it on your nose and on your mouth. We didn't know what it was, but that's what they told us to do. We had a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brook, like, like a water way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember a lot of soldiers being there and a lot of shooting the next day. Not the first day, the next day. A lot of-- we didn't know what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents say? What did they make of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nothing's going to happen. The Germans are advancing, but they'll pass by. Nothing's going to happen. This was the big problem, I think, now that I know better, that we were so passive.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- it's not going to happen to us. Nothing's going to happen to us. Don't worry, God is here. He's going to help us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a few days later, we saw Germans in town, soldiers on bicycles and on horses. How were they dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Green uniforms with their helmets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Green?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's like darkish green.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: With their helmets and riding on horses and bicycles and cars. I never saw a car like that before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it wasn't bad the first few days. We lived, it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't go to any school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were rumors of being beaten and things like that, but that was for everybody, not just Jews. It was everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't go out in the streets. But as little kids, we went out. We went around among the soldiers. You and your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It wasn't that bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then it got worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we-- we had to move in into-- they called it a ghetto. It was a-- they took like a-- a few square blocks. Now, I saw it, so I know what it-- where it was. But at that time, I didn't. Where all the Jews from that town had to move in into that square, period. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They gave a proclamation and that's it, you had to go. You didn't ask why, where, or what. You just went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my mother took the bedding, so we can have something to sleep on. But that's all. I don't remember ever taking any clothes. First of all, we didn't have that much. But I don't remember taking anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or anything precious to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If you had-- I remember when we were taken in there, there were guards on the side. And they would take the belongings away anyway, so there was nothing left. They just took it. It was the right, I guess, of the German people to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took your bedding away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the bedding they didn't. But everything else that people carried, they just said leave it here and go-- go in there. You couldn't go out to work, there was no work. Couldn't go to school. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just-- in that little thing, there was a bakery. And you couldn't do much. You couldn't do anything. Food was a little bit available, as much as for everybody else at that time. And we would go to the villages to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to get some food together and bring it back. Everybody did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just we kids. It was harder for grown ups to go, especially if they had payots. It was harder. So we the kids just went, my sister's, then I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still had your payots?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. When we went in-- into-- into that little ghetto, my mother cut them. I guess part of life, again. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you remember what you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: If my mother did it, it was right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How can I question it? I didn't know even how to question. My mother said, the Germans are here. They're going to beat you, I guess. Let's cut the curls. And we did it. A lot of people did it. But my father was about to die. He didn't do it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he didn't do it. And I remember there was a streetcar-- a train, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: On rails?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rails--

speaker: interviewer
text: Railroad car, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --passing by about two blocks from where we stayed in that little ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after a while, there were less Jews and less Jews and less Jews in that little ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the mayor of the town, I guess, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or the Germans-- I don't know who it was. But they had to fill a certain boxcar of people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this-- and this was-- I think it was already in-- in '40. I don't remember. But we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? You just saw that less and less--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taking the people. They told us they're taking them to-- to workplaces where they can get work and make money and live. When they said, we need 100 more, nobody cared. Because you'll go to work and live like a human being. You're going to be fed. And you're going to live in a place. So we just went. We didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I guess nobody knew. We ended up in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with the whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Warsaw ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your father and three sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, my three sisters, and I. At the beginning, they did not separate the people, the families, I guess. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how it was when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Warsaw ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the train ride, first of all, to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too long. It was like a-- it's 53 kilometers, now I know. It must have been an eternity, because to me as a, kid, being cooped up in a-- in a-- in a boxcar. But we were going to work. My father was going to go to work. And we're going to eat. And we're going to have a place to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were excited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, you were happy to go until you went in. And you went in and there was-- when you went out, you saw a fence. Now, I know how high it was. At that time, I thought-- I thought it was going to high heaven. It was this big fence with stuff on top of the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: With what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some barbed wires and glass. And the reason I know is later, when I climbed over, we were cut. But at the beginning, you didn't know. But you went in there, you know. And over here, you're going to work. And then you open your eyes, and then you see there's a fence, a block-filled fence. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's all you see. They put us--

speaker: interviewer
text: A block-filled fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Blocks in between, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, blocks like building blocks, construction blacks, not stones. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was all the way around. And we walked and walked and walked and walked. Must have been 1,000 miles. But-- but we walked to a place called Krochmalna. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's a little street in Warsaw in the ghetto. And the Judenrat, which was like a committee, would assign people to live, assign food coupons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got assigned to a room. There were a few other people in there at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: All six of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All six of us plus some other people that were in there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went in there. Pretty soon, there were more people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was an enormous amount of people in there. My sister tells me that there were 16 people. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one room. We all slept on the floor. There was no beds, no nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got food rations, which was-- I think it was a couple pieces of bread. I don't remember, because I was not going to get it. My father my mother usually did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they didn't eat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't eat it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] We ate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave it to the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You see, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I-- I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] towards-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were there quite a while, the ghetto, where my sister, who is now in Israel, had a baby. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were walking down the street, Krochmalna. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She and her husband, and she held the baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] And the gestapo men came by, grabbed the baby out of her hands, and hit it against the wall. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the baby died on the spot. I remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Telling your tales about the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] waking up one morning, slept with my father. And I still remember it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know when you put a foot over somebody? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's how I slept most of the time, put my foot over my father and slept all night. And I woke up in the morning, and he was dead. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember. [CRYING] We couldn't bury him. We didn't know where. We didn't know how. We didn't know what. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, the cemetery was in the big part of the Warsaw ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in the little part of the Warsaw ghetto. There was a bridge going through it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the people-- every morning, the same thing. They would take the dead people out and put them on the curb. And [NON-ENGLISH] would pick them up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what the [NON-ENGLISH] is? And they would pick them up and put them in a pushcart and take them away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Where? I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember going over to the big side, the big ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] There was a train coming in every single day. And every single day, people would go on the train. They would go to work. The train would come in empty. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That's when [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I decided, I guess, I don't know-- something told me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get out of here. [CRYING] I was a big guy by then. I was 13. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] In the wall, there was a-- on every corner, there was a square block on the bottom cut out so that the water from the drains can go through and run out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, being scrawny, little kids, as we were at that time, we would crawl out at night and run away. Go to the villages where you came from. Go to the fields. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Get a potato, a sugar beet, whatever you could. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Come back to the ghetto and feed your family. I had sisters and my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the man of the house. [CRYING] There was a streetcar about two blocks from where we lived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just saw it. The only thing left of the streetcar lines is about 10 feet of it. The street car, that would come in from one end, go around of the Jewish ghetto, and go out to the other side. Like, they didn't want to divert the travel line of the streetcar, so that they wouldn't go through the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we would jump on it. There were Polish police. There were Ukraine police. And there were German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Ukraines were worse than the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If they caught us, not only did they take everything away, they would beat the heck out of us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Polacks, sometimes they would keep it. And they would say, OK, what you got in your pockets? And if you had a zloty, you gave it to him, everything was fine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans relied on those, you see. if it wasn't for-- now, that I know better. If it wasn't for the Polish police and the Ukraine police guarding the ghetto, they would have to supply with more German soldiers. There would be less soldiers on the front, now that I think about it. But at that time I didn't think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was caught many a times, many a times. But that was just nothing. So you get beat up, so what? Big deal. You brought home some potatoes. That's a big deal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To get beat up was nothing. If they didn't shoot you, that was OK. But to get beat up, it was part of ghetto life. If you did not get beat up, you didn't belong there. I remember many a times we would jump on that street car and jump off the other side. Usually, the conductor of the street car would speed it up as he was passing through that corner of the ghetto. But we knew how to stop him. We put stones on the tracks and things like that. He had to slow down. We jumped on it. And if you were young, you spoke Polish and you did not have a Jewish accent. By that, I mean if you-- if you went to Polish school, you really didn't have it. You could pass. And I used to go to the villages and bring the stuff. And one day, I decided I was going to take my family out of there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I took my older sister, who's now in Israel--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bronya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bronya.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband, I took them both out on two separate trips. Because they-- she was a seamstress. And he was a tailor. So they could find hidden places in the-- at the farms of the peoples.

speaker: interviewer
text: And get work?

speaker: interviewee
text: And get work, because the farmers needed the work. So they would say, OK, you sleep in the barn and fix this. And after a while, it became dangerous for them. So she joined the Polish partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he?

speaker: interviewee
text: And him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirsh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hirsh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went for my second sister. I expected to take them all out. My second sister came out. And she was more of a rebel than a-- a person that gives in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: Esther. And how old was Esther now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She must've been-- well, she is 7 years old-- was 7 years old than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's 20.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a rebel. By that, I mean in those days, she even wore lipstick. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I took her to the farms, and she worked. And she never said that she's not Jewish. I remember when she-- she joined the [INAUDIBLE]. You know what the [INAUDIBLE] is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like a workmen's circle, but a little stronger. I remember that. And she was a young girl. And-- because she couldn't stand the people being stepped on. So she figured maybe she'll do something better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she pretty?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, they were all pretty. But honest, I don't remember. If you would show me a picture of her now, or of my father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] or my mother, I couldn't tell you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just couldn't. And I have no picture of them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Took her out to the villages. I think the place-- I just called my sister. And she told me the names of the place. And one of the places, [INAUDIBLE]. And the other one, I have it written down some place. And she worked. And she was fine for a while, until all the people knew that-- I guess an edict came out or whatever. I don't remember. I don't know. But if you turned in a Jew, dead or alive, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you got a bottle of vodka. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My sister was a bottle of vodka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] I saw it. I watched. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She saw me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bunch of Polacks just beating the hell out of her until she was dead. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in order to get the bottle of vodka, they had to go for the German to show them what they did to get the bottle of vodka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How to believe people would do that? Believe it. I was there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in one of the villages, either [INAUDIBLE] or the other one--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, outside? No, I don't mean-- outside in the streets, or where?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, we used to live like-- in Europe in those days, after they got all the wheat and stuff together, they would put it into a round pile, dome-like. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we would dig on the bottom at night and make a hole and crawl in there to sleep. So it was nice and warm. And you put the stuff back in there, so nobody saw that you are in there. And that's how we slept. We did not go to people's homes or barns, because they were scared, too. And we would be turned in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with Esther?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same village for a  while, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But sleeping in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't want to be caught together, because then both would be killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- yeah. [SIGHS]
